title: Expressing support for the designation of May 2, 2025, as "NKH Awareness Day".
plain_summary: This congressional resolution asks to make May 2, 2025 a special day called NKH Awareness Day. NKH is a very rare disease that happens when too much of a substance called glycine builds up in a person's body and brain. This disease affects about one in every 76,000 people around the world and mostly hurts how children grow and develop. The goal is to help more people learn about this rare condition so doctors can better find it and researchers can work on treatments.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Resolution Expressing support for the designation of May 2, 2025, as 'NKH Awareness Day'
sections.text: A rare disease is any disease that affects fewer than two hundred thousand people in the United States at any given time. Children make up more than half of the estimated twenty five million to thirty five million people in the United States living with a rare disease. Nonketotic hyperglycinemia is a rare genetic metabolic disorder. People call it NKH for short. In this disorder, unusually high levels of a substance called glycine build up in a patient's body. NKH is also known as glycine encephalopathy. NKH affects about one in seventy six thousand people worldwide. NKH happens because of a mutation in the glycine cleavage enzyme system. This means that patients cannot break down glycine properly. The glycine builds up throughout the body and especially in the spinal cord and brain. NKH has two forms called severe and attenuated NKH. Most children born with NKH do not reach typical developmental milestones. Some children who do reach these milestones may lose these abilities over time. Doctors can diagnose NKH by using a combination of a physical exam, biochemical testing, and genetic analysis. There are only about five hundred cases of NKH diagnosed worldwide. Four known patients living with NKH are in Virginia. Congress can raise awareness of NKH among the public and in the medical community of the United States. Increased awareness of NKH may result in more research, more participation in NKH patient registries, and more diagnoses of NKH cases. Therefore, the House of Representatives supports the designation of NKH Awareness Day and recognizes the importance of raising awareness and improving education around nonketotic hyperglycinemia.
